revision.text:
[[de:Buch vom Tage]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|qq=}}{{complex|qq=}} The term '''Book of the Day''' is the name of the only fragmentary title “Instructions for knowing the names of the hours of the day”. [[Altägyptische Sprache|Altägyptischer Titel]]: ''seschmu en-rech renu en na-wenut-net-heru'' gemäß Markus Müller-Roth: ''Das Buch vom Tage''. S. 61. As a book of heaven and {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=the afterlife,|1=Duat}} it contains the daily twelve-hour journey of the sun god {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Re|1=Ra}} in his barque, which lasts from {{ill|Sunrise|en|Sunrise|qid=Q193294|lt=Sunrise|1=Sunrise}} to {{ill|Sunset|en|Sunset|qid=Q166564|lt=Sunset|1=Sunset}} . == Origin == The book of the day is first published in the [[11 Kingdom of Egypt|20th. Dynasty]] in the {{ill|KV9|en|lt=tomb of Ramses VI|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KV9|qq=}} (1145 to 1137 v. Chr.) As {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=an ancient Egyptian|1=Ancient Egypt}} religious treatise, in contrast to the other books of the afterlife, it was not depicted on the burial chamber walls, but on the cosmologically designed ceilings. The [[:en:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]] are written entirely in yellow on a black background. Initially, the Book of the Day in combination with the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}} was reserved exclusively for the tombs of kings ( {{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|qid=Q37110|lt=pharaohs|1=Pharaoh}} ). Only from the 25th In the 1st Dynasty it first appears without reference to a nut book among non-royal persons. One of the {{ill|Innovation|en|Innovation|qid=Q174165|lt=innovations|1=Innovation}} made was the depiction on the tops of sarcophagus lids, which {{ill|Symbol|en|Symbol|qid=Q80071|lt=symbolized|1=Symbol}} the burial chamber ceilings of the kings. The outer sarcophagus thus functioned as a “burial chapel”. == Construction == The Book of the Day contains numerous older texts on various topics, some of which date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} . 11 {{ill|Theology|en|Theology|qid=Q34178|lt=theological|1=Theology}} interpretations are therefore not always easy to recognize. Each hour of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Day|lt=day|fr|Journée_égyptienne_antique|de|Tag_(Altes_Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Day|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Day|qid=Q1669420|1=Ancient Egyptian Day}} is assigned an {{ill|Hour deities|lt=Hour deity|de|Stundengottheiten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qid=Q2359142|1=Hour deities}} who exercises patronage over that hour. In contrast to the {{ill|Amduat|4=en|6=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|qid=Q460509|1=Amduat}} and the {{ill|Book of Gates|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Gates|qq=}}, the sky is depicted without boundaries. Re completes the sun's trajectory of the day in the sun barque, which moves along a heavenly water channel. {|class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" | colspan="2" align="center" style="background:#FFE39B" |'''Hours of the day and the corresponding hour deities''' |- bgcolor="#FFE39B" ! Hour ! Surname |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qid=Q1362046|1=First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)}} | ''That makes the beauty of Re appear (The hour that satisfies)'' ( [[:en:Ma'at|Maat]] or {{ill|Heka (god)|en|Heka (god)|qid=Q846113|lt=Heka|1=Heka (god)}} ) |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 2nd Hour | ''She who drives away the darkness'' ( {{ill|Hu (mythology)|lt=Hu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hu (mythology)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} ) |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 3rd Hour | ''Who makes the {{ill|Ba|en|Ba|qid=Q202341|lt=Bas|1=Ba}} of the gods rejoice and sees millions'' ( {{ill|Sia (god)|lt=Sia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sia (god)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} ) |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 4th Hour | ''Light of the Dawn'' ( [[Asebit]] ) |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 5th Hour | ''The Hour of the Goddess [[:en:Igeret|Igeret]]'' |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 6th Hour | ''She who rises in the grasp of {{ill|Set (deity)|en|Set (deity)|qid=Q131795|lt=Seth|1=Set (deity)}}'' |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 7th Hour | ''That makes the heart wide'' ( {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}} ) |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 8th Hour | ''Becoming and Emergence (Rejoicing of the gods over the defeat of [[:en:Apep|Apophis]] )'' ( {{ill|Khonsu|en|Khonsu|qid=Q190521|lt=Chons|1=Khonsu}} ) |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 9th Hour | ''Hour of translation to'' {{ill|Aaru|en|Aaru|qid=Q618140|lt=Sechet-iaru|1=Aaru}} ( {{ill|Isis|en|Isis|qid=Q79876|lt=Isis|1=Isis}} ) |- |10th Hour |''Descend to [[Seket-Barke|the Seket barge]] to cross'' ( [[Hike-wer]] ) |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 11th Hour | ''Straightening the ropes'' (He who straightens the ropes) |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |'''{{ill|twelfth Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/twelfth Hour (Ancient Egypt)|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}''' | ''Perishing of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ( {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Re|1=Ra}} ) |} == Related pages == * {{ill|Book of the Night|de|Buch der Nacht|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Night|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Night|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} == Literature == * [[Markus Müller-Roth]] : ''The book of the day'' . Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Göttingen 2008, ISBN 3-5255-3453-1 * [[Jürgen Osing]] : ''The hours of day and night'' . In: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3, p. 198–201. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 == References == {{translated page|de|Buch vom Tage|version=189562676|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Buch vom Tage]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day]] {{wikidata link|Q997784}}
